meta.title: 2.5 Protocols
meta.description: HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3, WebSockets, Server-Sent Events, gRPC, and choosing the right protocol.

blocks.prompt: A chat application, a video streaming service, a REST API, and a real-time stock ticker each need to communicate between client and server. Would you use the same protocol for all four? What properties does each need?

blocks.content:
## HTTP evolution

### HTTP/1.1
One request per TCP connection at a time. Browsers work around this by opening 6 connections per domain (head-of-line blocking). Text-based headers sent with every request.

### HTTP/2
- **Multiplexing:** Multiple requests over one TCP connection simultaneously
- **Header compression (HPACK):** Dramatically reduces header overhead
- **Server push:** Server can proactively send resources before client requests them
- **Binary framing:** More efficient than text-based HTTP/1.1

Result: ~50% faster page loads. Adopted by 97%+ of browsers.

### HTTP/3 (QUIC)
- Built on **UDP** instead of TCP
- **No head-of-line blocking:** Lost packets only affect their stream, not all streams
- **Faster connection setup:** 0-RTT resumption (first request in the first packet)
- **Built-in encryption:** TLS 1.3 integrated into the protocol

Result: Significant improvements on lossy networks (mobile, WiFi). Google reports 2% reduction in search latency globally.

## Beyond request-response

### WebSockets
Full-duplex, bidirectional communication over a single TCP connection. Client and server can send messages at any time.

- **Use for:** Chat, multiplayer games, collaborative editing, real-time dashboards
- **Tradeoffs:** Persistent connections consume server resources. Load balancing is harder (sticky sessions needed). Not cacheable.

### Server-Sent Events (SSE)
Server pushes updates to the client over a long-lived HTTP connection. Unidirectional (server → client only).

- **Use for:** News feeds, notifications, real-time stock prices, log streaming
- **Advantages over WebSockets:** Uses standard HTTP (works with existing infrastructure), automatic reconnection, simpler to implement
- **Tradeoff:** One-way only. If you need client → server, you still need regular HTTP requests

### gRPC
Google's RPC framework. Uses HTTP/2 for transport and Protocol Buffers for serialization.

- **Use for:** Service-to-service communication in microservices, mobile clients (bandwidth efficient)
- **Advantages:** 2-10x faster than JSON/REST, strongly typed contracts, streaming support (unary, server, client, bidirectional)
- **Tradeoffs:** Browser support requires gRPC-Web proxy, harder to debug (binary format), steeper learning curve

## Choosing the right protocol

| Scenario | Protocol | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Public REST API | HTTP/2 | Standard, cacheable, universal |
| Real-time chat | WebSockets | Bidirectional, low latency |
| Live notifications | SSE | Simple, unidirectional server push |
| Service-to-service | gRPC | Fast, typed, streaming |
| Mobile app API | HTTP/2 or gRPC | Bandwidth efficient |
| File upload | HTTP/2 | Chunked transfer, resumable |
| Video streaming | HTTP/2 + adaptive bitrate | CDN-friendly, standard |

problems.question: A fintech company needs to push real-time stock price updates to web browser clients. Updates are server-initiated (prices change, clients display them). Clients do not send data back during the stream. Which protocol is the best fit?
options: B) Server-Sent Events (SSE) — server-to-client push over HTTP
C) gRPC server streaming — efficient binary streaming
problems.explanation: SSE is the best fit because: (1) communication is unidirectional (server → client), matching SSE's model exactly; (2) SSE uses standard HTTP, so it works with existing CDNs, proxies, and load balancers; (3) SSE has built-in reconnection with last-event-ID; (4) it is simpler to implement than WebSockets. WebSockets (A) would work but is overkill — you don't need bidirectional communication. gRPC (C) doesn't work natively in browsers without a proxy. For a web browser use case with server-to-client push, SSE is the right tool.

blocks.content: HTTP/2 multiplexes requests over one connection; HTTP/3 (QUIC) eliminates head-of-line blocking on UDP. WebSockets enable bidirectional real-time communication. SSE is simpler for server-to-client push. gRPC excels at service-to-service communication. Choose the protocol that matches your communication pattern.

blocks.prompt: You now know the protocols. Module 2.6 puts it all together — designing for global delivery with anycast, multi-region deployment, and edge computing.
